coredata.dc:title: Engineering a pH-responsive fluorometric system: Dual-emission carbon dots and BTB for selective urea sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
Elmasry, Mohamed R.
Bin Jardan, Yousef A.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: Reliable detection of urea levels is critical for tracking kidney health, identifying diseases, and studying essential physiological functions. Here, we present a novel, straightforward, and selective fluorometric method for urea detection based on a dual-emission carbon dots system coupled with bromothymol blue (BTB) as a pH-sensitive indicator. The sensing platform consists of uniquely engineered carbon dots exhibiting dual emission peaks at 442 nm and 612 nm, working in conjunction with BTB in the presence of urease. The detection mechanism relies on urease-catalyzed hydrolysis of urea, which generates ammonia and subsequently increases the local pH. This pH elevation triggers a distinct color transformation of BTB from yellow (430 nm) to blue (615 nm), simultaneously causing a drop in fluorescence intensity at 612 nm and a rise at 442 nm, enabling ratiometric detection of urea. The developed method demonstrates excellent analytical performance with good linearity (R2 = 0.9937) across a concentration range of 10.0–100.0 μM and achieves a detection limit of 2.86 μM. Notably, the method exhibits remarkable selectivity in complex biological matrices, successfully quantifying urea in human serum samples with high recovery values despite the presence of potential interferents. Furthermore, we extended the application of this sensing system to develop a smartphone-based colorimetric detection platform, enhancing its accessibility for point-of-care diagnostics. This dual-mode detection method provides a fast and dependable tool for urea monitoring in clinical and biological applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Urea
Carbon dots
Bromothymol blue
Ratiometric sensing
Smartphone-based detection

Detection of urea in human serum samples
Colorimetric detection of urea using smartphone
Morphological and structural characterization of BO@CDs
Selectivity and anti-interference study of urea biosensor
Determination of urea via smartphone platform
Determination of urea in human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM micrograph with corresponding particle size distribution, (B) XRD profile, (C) FTIR spectrum, and (D) XPS data for BO@CDs.

(A) UV–vis, photoluminescence, and excitation spectra of BO@CDs with the characteristic emission at 442 nm and 615 nm (red line) after excitation at 275 nm (blue line). (B) Fluorescence spectrum of BO@CDs when excited from 260 to 300 nm.
(A) The effect of various concentrations of urea (0.0–100.0 μM) on the emission intensity of BO@CDs/BTB system. (B) Relationship of (F442/F612) against concentration (10.0–100.0 μM) of urea.

(A) Emission spectra overlay of BO@CDs, BO@CDs + BTB (pH 6.0), and BO@CDs + BTB + urea. (B) Comparison of BO@CDs photoluminescence spectra with BTB UV–Vis absorption spectra at pH 6.0, both with and without urea. (C) Influence of varying urea concentrations (10.0–100.0 μM) on the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of BTB. (D) Fluorescence lifetime analysis of BO@CDs in the presence and absence of BTB.
Linear dependence of the B/R ratio in BTB images on urea concentration when exposed to visible light.
A comparison of the designed method with other previously reported fluorescent probes for urea sensing.

row.entry: Pool water, lake water and mineral water samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of the analysis of urea in human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Engineering a pH-responsive fluorometric system: Dual-emission carbon dots and BTB for selective urea sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reliable detection of urea levels is critical for tracking kidney health, identifying diseases, and studying essential physiological functions. Here, we present a novel, straightforward, and selective fluorometric method for urea detection based on a dual-emission carbon dots system coupled with bromothymol blue (BTB) as a pH-sensitive indicator. The sensing platform consists of uniquely engineered carbon dots exhibiting dual emission peaks at 442 nm and 612 nm, working in conjunction with BTB in the presence of urease. The detection mechanism relies on urease-catalyzed hydrolysis of urea, which generates ammonia and subsequently increases the local pH. This pH elevation triggers a distinct color transformation of BTB from yellow (430 nm) to blue (615 nm), simultaneously causing a drop in fluorescence intensity at 612 nm and a rise at 442 nm, enabling ratiometric detection of urea. The developed method demonstrates excellent analytical performance with good linearity (R2 = 0.9937) across a concentration range of 10.0–100.0 μM and achieves a detection limit of 2.86 μM. Notably, the method exhibits remarkable selectivity in complex biological matrices, successfully quantifying urea in human serum samples with high recovery values despite the presence of potential interferents. Furthermore, we extended the application of this sensing system to develop a smartphone-based colorimetric detection platform, enhancing its accessibility for point-of-care diagnostics. This dual-mode detection method provides a fast and dependable tool for urea monitoring in clinical and biological applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Urea is a vital nitrogen-containing compound formed in the liver as the product of protein and amino acid metabolism, playing a crucial role in the body's nitrogen excretion process. It is primarily excreted by the kidneys, making it a key marker for assessing renal function and overall metabolic health [1]. In healthy individuals, normal urea levels in human serum typically range between7 to 20 mg/dL(or 2.5 to 7.1 mmol/L), though these values may vary slightly depending on age, diet, and laboratory standards [2]. Abnormal urea levels, whether elevated or reduced, can indicate underlying health issues; high levels may suggest kidney dysfunction, dehydration, or excessive protein breakdown, while low levels may point to liver disease, malnutrition, or overhydration [3]. The determination of urea in human serum is essential for diagnosing and monitoring conditions such as renal failure, liver disease, and metabolic disorders, as well as for evaluating the effectiveness of treatments and interventions. Accurate and timely measurement of urea levels is therefore critical in clinical practice for maintaining patient health and managing disease progression.

Previous analytical methods for urea detection include colorimetric assays [4], electrochemical sensors [5,6], and chromatographic techniques such as HPLC [7,8]. While colorimetric methods offer simplicity, they often suffer from poor sensitivity and are prone to interference from sample matrix components. Electrochemical approaches face challenges with electrode fouling and surface passivation in complex biological samples, while chromatographic methods require expensive instrumentation, complex sample preparation, and longer analysis times. In contrast, fluorometric techniques offer several distinct advantages [9,10]: superior sensitivity allowing detection at nanomolar levels, high selectivity through specific molecular interactions, rapid response times, and minimal sample preparation requirements. Furthermore, fluorescence-based methods enable real-time monitoring, require small sample volumes, and can be easily adapted for point-of-care diagnostics. The ratiometric fluorescence approach provides enhanced accuracy through self-calibration, effectively minimizing environmental interferences and instrumental fluctuations that commonly affect single-channel detection methods [11].

The enzymatic hydrolysis of urea by urease represents a fundamental reaction mechanism widely exploited in analytical chemistry. When urease catalyzes urea decomposition, it produces two molecules of ammonia and one molecule of carbon dioxide [12]. This reaction leads to significant changes in the chemical environment, particularly an increase in pH due to ammonia formation, which can be monitored through various detection techniques [13,14]. The specificity of urease for urea, combined with the substantial pH change accompanying the reaction, enables sensitive and selective analytical methods. These include colorimetric assays based on pH indicators [15], electrochemical measurements tracking ammonia production, and fluorescence-based detection systems monitoring pH-dependent fluorescence changes [16].

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as powerful fluorescent probes for analytical detection due to their unique photophysical properties and versatile surface chemistry [17]. These nanomaterials exhibit excellent photostability, tunable emission characteristics, high quantum yields, and exceptional biocompatibility, making them ideal for sensing applications [18]. Their surface can be readily functionalized with various recognition elements to achieve selective detection of target analytes, while their intrinsic fluorescence properties enable sensitive detection through different mechanisms including fluorescence enhancement, quenching, or ratiometric changes [19]. The facile and cost-effective synthesis of CDs, combined with their low toxicity and resistance to photobleaching, Provides notable benefits compared to conventional organic fluorophores and semiconductor quantum dots. Additionally, their excitation-dependent emission properties allow for multiplexed detection, while their robust nature enables reliable performance in complex biological matrices [20]. The integration of CDs into sensing platforms has revolutionized fluorescence-based detection by providing highly sensitive, selective, and environmentally friendly analytical tools for various biomedical and environmental applications.

This work presents a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform that uniquely combines dual-emission carbon dots (blue and orange emission carbon dots (BO@CDs)) with bromothymol blue for selective urea detection. The sensing mechanism cleverly exploits the urease-catalyzed hydrolysis of urea, which generates ammonia and subsequently increases the solution pH. This pH change triggers a systematic spectral transformation in bromothymol blue, characterized by a concurrent decrease at 430 nm and increase at 615 nm in its absorption spectrum. The innovative aspect lies in how these spectral changes effectively modulate the dual-emission properties of the carbon dots, resulting in an inverse relationship between their emission peaks at 442 nm and 612 nm. This double-signal modulation enables highly reliable ratiometric detection, as the two emission peaks serve as self-calibrating references, effectively minimizing environmental interferences and instrumental fluctuations. The method distinguishes itself through its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and practical applicability, demonstrated through successful validation in complex human serum matrices. This integration of pH-responsive dual-emission carbon dots with a classical pH indicator represents a significant advancement in ratiometric fluorescence sensing, offering a robust analytical platform that combines the sensitivity of fluorescence detection with the reliability of ratiometric measurements. The recent advancements in smartphone technology have enabled their integration into analytical methods, offering powerful imaging capabilities and data processing through various applications [21,22]. To leverage these capabilities, we developed a smartphone-based colorimetric detection platform utilizing the visible color changes of our sensing system, which provides rapid, quantitative analysis of urea levels through simple image capture and RGB value determination.

Urea, ascorbic acid, creatinine, glucose, glycine, histidine, uric acid, boric acid, bilirubin, Bovine serum albumin (BSA), calcium chloride, ornithine, allantoin, cholesterol, potassium chloride, sodium chloride, and citrulline were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Magnesium chloride, sodium pyruvate, sodium sulfate, sodium citrate, sodium lactate, sodium phosphate, phosphoric acid, sodium acetate, bromothymol blue (BTB), glacial acetic acid, o-phenylenediamine, and malonic acid were sourced from Sigma–Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). All experiments were conducted using ultra-pure water, which had a resistivity of 18. MΩ.cm.
Comprehensive details regarding the equipment and instruments utilized can be found in the Supplementary Data file.

The synthesis of BO@CDs was conducted using a previously reported one-pot solvothermal method, utilizing malonic acid and o-phenylenediamine as the carbon and nitrogen precursors, respectively [21]. In this process, accurately weighed amounts of malonic acid (270 mg) and o-PD (650 mg) were dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol, which served as the reaction medium. The resulting homogeneous solution was subjected to thermal treatment in a sealed Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 8 h to ensure complete carbonization. Following the reaction, the system was allowed to cool naturally to ambient temperature. The crude product was then purified through a two-step process: initially, large particulates were removed via membrane filtration (0.22 μm), followed by extensive dialysis against ultrapure water using a 1000 Da molecular weight cutoff membrane for 12 h to eliminate residual unreacted precursors and small molecular byproducts. For long-term preservation, the purified BO@CDs solution was maintained at 4 °C until additional experimentation.
The methodology and computational steps for calculating the quantum yield are thoroughly outlined in the Supplementary Data file.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of urea using BO@CDs /BTB sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitative analysis of urea was conducted through a systematic multi-step approach. Initially, 100 μL of urea solutions at varying concentrations were added to a urease enzyme solution (1.0 mg mL−1) prepared in a 10 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) using 10.0 mL volumetric flasks. The enzymatic hydrolysis of urea was carried out by incubating the reaction mixture at a physiological temperature of 37 °C for 30 min to ensure complete conversion. Upon completion of the enzymatic reaction, the sensing components were introduced sequentially: 100 μL of BO@CDs solution (100.0μg mL−1) followed by 100 μL of BTB indicator solution (500.0μg mL−1). The mixture was maintained at room temperature for one minute to ensure equilibration before adjusting the final volume with phosphate buffer (pH 6.0). Fluorescence measurements were subsequently performed, with excitation at 275 nm to obtain emission spectra at 442 nm and 612 nm, facilitating the quantitative determination of urea concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of urea in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical validation of the BO@CDs/BTB detection system was conducted using serum samples collected from three healthy male donors residing in different regions of Assiut Governorate, Egypt. The sample preparation process involved a series of sequential separation and purification steps to ensure accuracy and reliability. At the start, blood samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to obtain the serum fraction. A 0.5 mL aliquot of the isolated serum was then treated with 2.0 mL of acetonitrile for protein precipitation, followed by thorough mixing via vortex agitation for 5 min. A second centrifugation step, conducted under the same conditions, was employed to remove the precipitated proteins. The resulting clear supernatant was subsequently diluted (1:10) with ultrapure water to prepare it for analysis. To assess the method’s accuracy and precision, recovery studies were conducted by spiking the processed serum samples with known concentrations of urea before measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of urea using smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: Our experimental setup utilized an Oppo Reno 7 smartphone equipped with advanced imaging features, including a high-resolution 64-megapixel camera, a 26-mm focal length, autofocus, and digital image stabilization. For colorimetric analysis, we used a freely available Android application called Color Detector to perform quantitative measurements of color intensity. The experimental procedure involved preparing urea standard solutions in glass cuvettes, which were carefully shielded on all sides except the front-facing surface to maintain consistent and controlled lighting conditions. A calibration curve was constructed using urea concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 10.0 mM, following the detailed protocol described in section 2.5 of our methodology. During data acquisition, RGB (Red, Green, Blue) values were measured in triplicate for each standard solution using the Color Detector app. To identify the most reliable analytical signal, we evaluated the coefficient of determination (R2) between the urea concentrations and various color parameters across multiple color spaces, including RGB, CMY (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow), HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value), XYZ (CIE 1931 color space), Lab* (CIELAB color space), and LCh (Lightness, Chroma, hue). This comprehensive evaluation ensured a robust and versatile approach to colorimetric analysis. To maintain consistency, the smartphone camera was positioned at a fixed distance of 10 cm from each sample during image capture, ensuring uniform and reproducible imaging conditions. Additionally, all measurements were conducted under control ambient lighting to minimize external interference. This meticulous approach allowed us to develop a reliable, precise, and user-friendly method for urea quantification using smartphone-based colorimetric detection, demonstrating its potential for point-of-care applications and field use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of BO@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological and structural properties of the synthesized carbon dots were analyzed using advanced characterization methods. TEM imaging (Fig. 1 A) demonstrated that the nanoparticles exhibited a primarily spherical shape with high dispersion quality, confirming their successful synthesis without notable aggregation. The size distribution analysis, depicted in the inset of Fig. 1A, indicated that the nanoparticles ranged in diameter from 2.1 to 5.2 nm, with an average size of 4.1 nm. The structural organization of the carbon dots was further examined using XRD (Fig. 1B) [19], which revealed a broad diffraction peak centered at 25.68°. This pattern, characterized by a diffuse and wide peak without sharp crystalline features, strongly suggests an amorphous structure, indicating the absence of long-range atomic order typically found in crystalline materials. The FTIR spectrum offers a comprehensive analysis of the functional groups present on the surface of the carbon dots (Fig. 1C). The broad peak observed at 3294 cm−1, accompanied by a shoulder at 3184 cm−1, is related to O-H and N-H stretching oscillations, signifying the existence of hydroxyl groups and primary amines [23]. A faint absorption at 2928 cm−1 is linked to C-H stretching in aliphatic functionality. Prominent bands at 1644 cm−1 and 1593 cm−1 are indicative of C=O stretching in carbonyl/carboxyl groups and C=C stretching in aromatic groups [24], respectively. Peaks at 1505 cm−1 and 1477 cm−1 are characteristic of C=N stretching and C-H bending vibrations [25]. The band at 1272 cm−1 reflects C-N stretching in aromatic amines, while the peak at 1031 cm−1 linked to C-O stretching oscillations. Additionally, peaks at 830 cm−1 and 757 cm−1 suggest C-H out-of-plane bending vibrations in aromatic rings. This spectral analysis validates the incorporation of various oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots, which significantly influence their optical properties and chemical behavior. XPS analysis offers detailed insights into the surface chemical structure and bonding configurations of the carbon dots. The survey scan reveals three distinct peaks at 284.31, 396.49, and 532.87 eV, attributed to the C1s, N1s, and O1s core levels, respectively. This confirms the successful incorporation of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen within the material (Fig. 1D). High-resolution deconvolution of the C1s spectrum reveals three distinct carbon environments: the primary peak at 284.72 eV represents sp2 and sp3 C-C bonds forming the core structure of the carbon dots, the peak at 285.24 eV signifies C-N/C-O single bonds resulting from surface functionalization, and the peak at 289.12 eV corresponds to oxidized carbon species (C=O/O-C=O) (Fig. S1A). The N1s region features two peaks: one at 400.03 eV, attributed to pyrrolic or amine nitrogen (C-N), and another at 401.75 eV, indicating graphitic nitrogen integrated into the carbon framework (Fig. S1B). The O1s spectrum shows two oxygen environments with peaks at 532.16 eV and 532.75 eV, assigned to C=O (carbonyl) and C-O (hydroxyl) groups, respectively, highlighting the variety of oxygen-containing functional groups present on the carbon dot surface (Fig. S1C) [26].

The zeta potential (ζ) of BO@CDs was measured to be −12.1 mV (Fig. S2). This negative zeta potential indicates the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CDs, which play a crucial role in enhancing their dispersibility in water through the formation of hydrogen bonds with water molecules [27].

The synthesized carbon dots' optical features were examined utilizing UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. UV–visible spectroscopy identified three distinct absorption peaks at 234, 275, and 291 nm (Fig. 2 A). The signal at 234 nm is linked to π–π* transitions within aromatic C=C bonds, while the peaks at 275 and 291 nm are likely associated with n–π* transitions involving carbonyl (C=O) or other functional groups containing heteroatoms [28]. These absorption features suggest the presence of both aromatic structures and functionalized surface groups on the carbon dots. Fluorescence spectroscopy revealed two distinct emission bands at 442 and 612 nm when excited at 275 nm. The emission wavelengths, notably, did not change within the excitation range of 260–300 nm (Fig. 2B), indicating a luminescence behavior independent of excitation. However, variations in excitation energy influenced the emission intensity. For subsequent experiments, an excitation wavelength of 275 nm was selected as it produced the most intense fluorescence at 610 nm. Additionally, a quantum yield of 22.32 % was observed for the carbon dots, underscoring their efficient fluorescence emission.

A comprehensive stability assessment of the synthesized BO@CDs was undertaken to evaluate their performance under diverse environmental challenges, using the photoluminescence emission intensity ratio as a key performance indicator. The pH stability investigation demonstrated remarkable resilience across a wide spectrum (pH 2–12, Fig. S3A), with particularly consistent performance observed in the pH range above 5, indicating the material's versatility for biological applications. When exposed to varying ionic concentrations (0.01–2.0 M, Fig. S3B), the carbon dots maintained their fluorescence characteristics, suggesting their robustness in complex ionic environments. The photostability evaluation revealed exceptional resistance to photobleaching, as evidenced by minimal changes in the fluorescence ratio during extended UV exposure periods up to 180 min (Fig. S3C). Thermal stability testing demonstrated consistent performance up to 50 °C, beyond which a progressive decrease in the fluorescence ratio was observed as temperatures approached 100 °C (Fig. S3D). These comprehensive stability studies collectively establish the carbon dots as a highly resilient fluorescent platform, capable of maintaining reliable performance across the diverse environmental conditions typically encountered in analytical applications.

To examine the impact of dilution on the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots, solutions with varying concentrations were prepared (Fig. S4). A gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed as the concentration of carbon dots was reduced. This decline can be attributed to the dilution effect, which reduces the number of fluorescent emitters in the solution, leading to a proportional decrease in overall emission. Additionally, the absence of significant shifts in emission wavelength suggests that the fluorescence decrease is primarily due to concentration-dependent photon absorption rather than aggregation or self-quenching effects [29].

The optimization of enzymatic urea hydrolysis focused on three critical parameters affecting ammonia generation efficiency. Studies of urease concentration (0.05–1.5 mg mL−1, Fig. S5A) identified 1.0 mg mL−1 as the optimal enzyme level for complete substrate conversion. Time-course analysis revealed that 30.0 min provided sufficient reaction duration for maximal fluorescence signal development (Fig. S5B). Temperature optimization demonstrated enhanced reaction kinetics between 10–40 °C, with 37 °C selected as optimal due to favorable enzymatic activity. Notable signal deterioration occurred above 50 °C, attributed to thermal denaturation of urease (Fig. S5C). These optimized conditions established fundamental parameters for efficient urea quantification through enzymatic hydrolysis and subsequent ammonia detection.

The comprehensive optimization of urea detection conditions using the BO@CDs/BTB sensing system involved systematic investigation of pH, buffer concentration, and reaction time. pH screening across the range of 3–11 revealed a critical relationship between pH and fluorescence response, with the F442/F612 ratio increasing progressively (Fig. S6A). This increase stems from the pH-dependent spectral transformation of BTB, where rising pH causes a decrease in absorbance at 430 nm and an increase at 615 nm, directly modulating the carbon dots' fluorescence peaks. The optimal pH selection was strategically nuanced: while an acidic environment ensures good BTB absorbance at 430 nm for quantitative analysis, excessive acidity would neutralize ammonia and compromise detection sensitivity. Consequently, pH 6.0 was identified as the ideal compromise, providing the most substantial response change. Buffer ionic strength optimization, conducted from 1 to 500 mM (Fig. S6B), demonstrated that 10 mM achieved the highest response, with higher ionic strengths progressively diminishing the signal due to buffer resistance to pH changes due to ammonia release. Reaction time evaluation indicated that merely 1.0 min was sufficient for complete reaction, highlighting the system's rapid and efficient detection mechanism (Fig. S6C). These meticulously optimized conditions collectively establish a robust and sensitive platform for urea quantification using the BO@CDs/BTB sensing system.

The narrow linear range (10.0–100.0 μM) of the BO@CDs/BTB sensing system can be explained by the complex enzymatic mechanism involved. The system relies on urease-catalyzed hydrolysis of urea to generate ammonia, which triggers pH changes affecting both BTB color and dual fluorescence emissions. At concentrations below 10.0 μM, insufficient ammonia is produced to cause significant pH shifts, resulting in unreliable signal changes. Above 100.0 μM, several limitations occur: (1) urease activity reaches saturation, causing non-linear ammonia production; (2) the buffer capacity is exceeded, creating an uncontrolled pH environment; (3) the pH-dependent color transition of BTB becomes complete, eliminating further measurable changes; and (4) the ratiometric relationship between the opposing “turn-on” (442 nm) and “turn-off” (612 nm) fluorescence signals loses proportionality at extreme pH values. This enzymatic-pH-optical cascade maintains linearity only within the specific concentration window where all components operate in balanced proportion. Although the linear range covers just one order of magnitude, it is tailored to the likely application, ensures high fidelity in that critical region, and avoids errors that can arise from overextending the dynamic range beyond the sensor’s optimal operating conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference study of urea biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical selectivity of the BO@CDs/BTB sensing system was rigorously evaluated against a comprehensive panel of potential interfering substances commonly found in biological matrices. The method demonstrated exceptional specificity for urea detection, as evidenced by the distinctive changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio (F442/F612) shown in Fig. S7A and Fig. S7B. When challenged with various biological molecules including ascorbic acid, creatinine, glucose, amino acids (glycine, histidine), uric acid, bilirubin, and proteins (BSA), as well as metabolites such as citrulline, ornithine, allantoin, and cholesterol, the system maintained its baseline response. Similarly, common physiological ions and salts including chlorides (potassium, sodium, calcium, magnesium), sulfates, citrates, pyruvates, lactates, and phosphates produced negligible interference with the fluorescence ratio. In contrast, the introduction of urea at 0.08 mM concentration triggered a significant and specific change in the F442/F612 ratio. This remarkable selectivity stems from the intrinsic molecular recognition properties of the BO@CDs/BTB system, coupled with the strategic choice of ratiometric fluorescence measurement, which effectively minimizes matrix effects and background interference. Such robust discrimination capability in complex biological matrices underscores the method's practical utility for accurate urea quantification in clinical samples.

We further evaluated the anti-interference capacity of the BO@CDs/BTB approach by examining its response to urea under the influence of various potential interferents—including ions and other biomolecules—previously identified in the selectivity study (Fig. S7C and D). The fluorescence readings remained largely unaffected by these substances, confirming that the method reliably resists external interference. Such robustness underscores the high specificity of the BO@CDs/BTB system for urea detection and supports consistent performance even in complex biological samples.

The BO@CDs/BTB urea sensing system operates at pH 6.0, where selective fluorescence changes occur. BTB interacts with BO@CDs, quenching the 442 nm emission while leaving the 612 nm emission largely unaffected. This differential response enables accurate urea detection. The introduction of urea induces a series of synchronized spectral changes, observable both visually and spectroscopically. In the presence of urease, urea undergoes enzymatic hydrolysis, generating ammonia and increasing the pH. This pH elevation results in a distinct color transition from yellow to blue, along with simultaneous yet opposite fluorescence responses: a significant recovery in emission at 442 nm and a notable reduction at 612 nm (Fig. 4 A).This bidirectional modulation of emission intensities establishes a sophisticated ratiometric detection platform, where each emission band serves a complementary analytical role – the 442 nm peak functioning as a “turn-on” sensor while the 612 nm peak operates as a “turn-off” probe. This dual-response mechanism creates an inherently self-calibrating analytical system, where the ratio of these opposing fluorescence changes provides a reliable and sensitive metric for urea quantification.

The mechanistic investigation of the BO@CDs/BTB sensing system was conducted through detailed spectroscopic studies incorporating UV–Vis absorption, photoluminescence, and time-resolved photoluminescence emission spectra. The absorption profile of BTB exhibited a prominent peak at 430 nm, characteristic of its yellow form, which demonstrated significant spectral overlap with the 442 nm emission band of BO@CDs. The system's response to urea was characterized by distinct spectral transformations in the BTB absorption profile: a progressive decline of the 430 nm peak coincided with the development of a new absorption band at 615 nm, signifying BTB's transition to its blue form. This newly formed absorption band at 615 nm showed direct overlap with the 612 nm emission peak of BO@CDs (Fig. 4B), establishing the spectroscopic basis for the ratiometric sensing mechanism.

The spectroscopic analysis provides compelling evidence for the pH-dependent color change of BTB induced by ammonia liberation during urea hydrolysis. The observed spectral shifts, specifically the simultaneous increase in absorbance at 615 nm and decrease at 430 nm with increasing urea concentration (Fig. 4C), directly correlate with the characteristic behavior of BTB as it transitions from its acidic to basic form. This complementary relationship between the two absorption peaks demonstrates the conversion of the protonated yellow form of BTB (λmax = 430 nm) to its deprotonated blue form (λmax = 615 nm) as ammonia released from urea hydrolysis raises the local pH. The fluorescence intensity changes mirror these absorption spectral shifts, confirming that the observed fluorescent signal truly reflects the pH-dependent structural changes of BTB rather than other potential interfering factors. This dual confirmation through both absorption and fluorescence measurements strongly validates that the sensor response is indeed based on the specific interaction between BTB and the ammonia produced during urea decomposition, making it a reliable method for monitoring urea concentration.

To further investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, we explored two possible pathways: inner filter effect (IFE) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [39]. A comprehensive analysis was needed to determine which mechanism dominated in the BO@CDs/BTB system. Our time-resolved fluorescence measurements provided crucial insights into the underlying quenching process. The time-resolved photoluminescence emission spectra of BO@CDs—with and without BTB—were examined, as shown in Fig. 4D. A tri-exponential model (R2 tri = 0.999) provided a superior fit over the bi-exponential approach (R2 bi = 0.997), revealing three distinct emissive sites [40]. For BO@CDs, the decay components were 10.85 ± 0.20 ns, 4.70 ± 0.33 ns, and 2.20 ± 0.23 ns. When BTB was introduced, the decay components shifted to 8.83 ± 0.41 ns, 4.75 ± 0.42 ns, and 2.00 ± 0.31 ns. From these components, the average fluorescence lifetime of BO@CDs alone was calculated to be 8.15 ± 0.40 ns. In contrast, the presence of BTB reduced the average lifetime to 6.72 ns. This decrease suggests that BTB interacts with BO@CDs—potentially through an energy transfer mechanism such as FRET—thereby altering the emission properties of the carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of urea via smartphone platform
ce:section.ce:para: We engineered a smartphone-based detection platform for urea quantification leveraging the BO@CDs/BTB sensing system, which capitalizes on the visible color transformations occurring at different urea concentrations. This innovative approach surpasses traditional visual assessments by offering superior accuracy, heightened sensitivity, and the ability to digitally document results. The experimental protocol involves smartphone camera imaging of the detection system under ultraviolet illumination, where solution colors progressively transition from yellow to green to blue with increasing urea concentration. By utilizing a Color Detector application, we extracted precise RGB color values that quantitatively represent these chromatic shifts. The analytical results demonstrated a robust linear relationship between color changes and urea concentration across the 0.1–10.0 mM range (Fig. 5 ), with an impressive limit of detection at 0.023 mM. Importantly, this method shows promising potential for analyzing urea in complex biological samples, including serum, urine, and cell culture media, with reliable detection capabilities across physiologically relevant concentration ranges. The visible light image analysis revealed a remarkably strong linear correlation between the blue/red (B/R) ratio and urea concentration, characterized by the equation B/R = 0.55[urea] + 0.33, with an exceptional correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9987). This smartphone-integrated approach represents a key breakthrough in decentralized testing, merging portable device accessibility with the high analytical performance of our dual-emission BO@CDs/BTB sensor to enable precise diagnostics outside traditional clinical environments. The approach offers a practical, rapid, and accessible solution for urea detection across various biomedical and environmental monitoring contexts, potentially revolutionizing on-site analytical capabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of urea in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess our urea detection method's real-world utility, we conducted comprehensive validation testing using clinical human serum samples. This involved spiking known concentrations of urea into serum specimens and calculating recovery rates to assess the method's accuracy and reliability. The results demonstrated high precision and consistency. Measured urea concentrations in the serum samples closely matched established reference ranges, as detailed in Table 2 . Notably, the method achieved average recovery rates ranging from 98.43 % to 99.40 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 3.63 %, highlighting its robustness and reliability in analyzing complex biological samples. To further validate the method, we compared its performance with a well-established HPLC detection technique from the literature [7]. A high degree of agreement was found between the two methods, with recovery rates showing no statistically significant variations. This strong correlation confirms the accuracy and reproducibility of our approach, particularly in handling the challenges of urea quantification in complex biological matrices like human serum. Successful validation highlights the potential of our smartphone-based colorimetric method as a practical, accessible, and reliable tool for urea detection. Its simplicity and accuracy make it a promising candidate for point-of-care diagnostics and biomedical research applications. Additionally, the method's cost-effectiveness and ease of use could facilitate widespread adoption in resource-limited settings, further enhancing its utility in healthcare and research.

The development of a novel, dual-emission carbon dots-based fluorometric sensing system coupled with bromothymol blue demonstrates significant potential for selective and sensitive urea detection. The strategic integration of pH-responsive BTB with dual-emitting carbon dots creates a robust ratiometric detection platform that effectively minimizes environmental interferences and measurement uncertainties. Our system's ability to exhibit clear spectral changes through both fluorometric and colorimetric responses provides versatile detection options, while the successful implementation of a smartphone-based detection platform significantly enhances its practical utility for point-of-care applications. The method's excellent analytical performance, characterized by good linearity, low detection limit, and high selectivity in complex biological matrices, particularly in human serum samples, underscores its potential for clinical diagnostics. The successful validation against established methods and impressive recovery values in real sample analysis further confirms the reliability and practicality of this approach. Moreover, the simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and user-friendly nature of this detection system make it particularly attractive for routine urea monitoring in both clinical and research settings. These findings pave the way for the development of accessible, reliable, and efficient tools for urea detection, with broad implications for healthcare diagnostics and biological monitoring applications.

Al-Montaser Bellah H. Ali: Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Mohamed R. Elmasry: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Yousef A. Bin Jardan: Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for funding this work through the project number (RSP2025R457).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2025.116416.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
